reasoning: The injustice is obvious because the enforcer (property manager) is openly violating the very rule they are using to evict the user. A reasonable person would immediately recognize the hypocrisy and unfairness of evicting a tenant for a quiet cat while the manager keeps a Great Dane on the premises.

Penalizing a tenant for failing to pay via a specific portal when that portal is demonstrably broken constitutes an obvious injustice. No reasonable person would defend the imposition of a late fee when the landlord's own infrastructure prevents compliance.

The frequency (six times in two months) and timing (7 AM) of the inspections, combined with the fact that neighbors are completely exempt, constitutes a clear violation of the tenant's right to quiet enjoyment. No reasonable person would consider bi-weekly early morning inspections standard or fair landlord behavior without an active emergency, making the weaponization of the rule obvious.

Penalizing a tenant for a noise violation when they have objective proof they were in a different state is obviously unjust. A reasonable person would immediately agree that refusing to review evidence of factual impossibility because 'the system is automated' constitutes a gross misapplication of authority.

Changing enforcement criteria overnight without notifying the subjects, followed immediately by severe penalties (towing), is a textbook example of unfair application. No reasonable person would defend towing a tenant for failing to display a permit they were never told they needed.

A 400% bill increase accompanied by a refusal to provide the underlying data (meter reading) or calculation is obviously unjust. No reasonable person would defend a landlord's right to demand payment while hiding the basis for the charge, making the application of the billing rule arbitrary and opaque.

The injustice is obvious because the enforcement is demonstrably selective and arbitrary. A reasonable person would agree that penalizing the user for a privacy screen while ignoring neighbors' blatant violations of the same rule (a rusty bike and a massive banner) is unfair.

The injustice is obvious because the user is being penalized for past conduct that was explicitly authorized by the property manager at the time. A reasonable person would view evicting a currently compliant tenant for actions they were told were permissible as a breach of trust and unfair retroactive enforcement.

The case clearly demonstrates selective enforcement of a 'dead letter' rule. A reasonable person would agree that suddenly enforcing a strict guest policy against one tenant via surveillance, while knowingly allowing all other tenants to violate it for years, is an unfair application of authority.

The application of the late fee rule is obviously unjust because the landlord has made compliance impossible by closing early without notice and failing to maintain the drop box. No reasonable person would argue a tenant should pay an extra month's rent when they attempted to return keys on time but were physically prevented by the landlord's own negligence.

The injustice is obvious because the landlord is enforcing a rule against the user while openly tolerating flagrant violations by other tenants, such as waving at a neighbor's Great Dane. This selective enforcement, particularly when linked to a recent maintenance complaint, constitutes a clear abuse of authority that a reasonable person would immediately recognize as unfair.

The injustice is obvious and indisputable: the authority mandates a specific method of compliance (online portal) while failing to provide that method (server error), yet threatens an automatic penalty. No reasonable person would defend penalizing a tenant for the landlord's own infrastructure failure when the tenant is actively trying to pay.

Penalizing a resident for violating a rule change (permit color) that was never communicated to them is procedurally unjust on its face. No reasonable person would defend booting a paying tenant's car for failing to comply with a secret administrative change.

Four inspections in a single month for generic 'safety checks' while neighbors receive zero is a clear violation of the standard covenant of quiet enjoyment. No reasonable person would defend weekly invasive inspections of a single tenant without a specific, stated emergency or maintenance cause.

The application of a penalty for conduct the user demonstrably did not commit (proven by plane tickets), combined with a refusal to review evidence and direct seizure of funds, is obviously unjust. No reasonable person would defend a system where a landlord can arbitrarily fine a tenant who wasn't even on the premises without any mechanism for correction.

The injustice is clear because the landlord is enforcing a new rule retroactively on previously compliant property with an unreasonably short deadline (48 hours). A reasonable person would agree that while rules can change, demanding immediate removal of established fixtures without a grandfather clause or adequate notice period is an unfair application of authority.

The landlord's demand conflicts with a higher legal authority (the ADA) and requires documentation that does not officially exist. A reasonable person would immediately recognize that conditioning housing on the production of non-existent, legally prohibited paperwork is an unjust application of authority.

A reasonable person could argue that the landlord is simply enforcing a valid lease term the user agreed to. The defense 'everyone else is doing it' is not strong enough to make the enforcement obviously unjust without proof that the landlord explicitly permits it for others while targeting the user (as opposed to just not catching them yet).
suggested_fix: Make the selective enforcement explicit and acknowledged by the authority. Example: 'The landlord sent a memo stating the guest policy is suspended, but is still using it to issue warnings specifically to me.'

reasoning: A reasonable person would immediately agree that charging a tenant a variable, unusually high utility fee ($200+) while refusing to disclose the meter readings or calculation method is fundamentally unfair. Transparency in billing is a basic expectation in housing contracts, and the landlord's refusal to provide proof of usage makes the charge arbitrary and potentially fraudulent.

The application of the rule is obviously unjust because compliance is rendered impossible by the landlord's failure to maintain the drop box. No reasonable person would defend penalizing a tenant $2,000 for failing to return keys when the designated return mechanism was physically blocked.
